entries.title: Client-Side Path Traversal - the essence of the vulnerability
entries.body:
CSPT (Client-Side Path Traversal), sometimes "On-site Request Forgery", is a bug that is used as a tool for CSRF or XSS attacks.

The essence: client-side code makes a request via fetch to a URL whose path includes controllable input. If the input is not encoded, you can inject "../" into the path - after the browser normalizes it, the request goes to a different URL.

Since the request is initiated from the application's own frontend, the browser automatically attaches cookies and other authentication - and they become available for exploitation.

entries.title: Where to look for CSPT
entries.body:
• Any client-side fetch / XHR where the URL path is built from user input (query parameter, fragment, form field).
• An "identifier" parameter that the frontend inserts into the path of an API request (newsitemid, email, inviteCode, channelname, etc.).
• Places where input is NOT URL-encoded before being inserted into the path - that is where "../" will slip through.
• The goal is to make the request go to an arbitrary endpoint on the same origin, to which the browser itself will attach cookies / CSRF token.

entries.title: CSPT to XSS - technique of manipulating the path in fetch
entries.body:
After loading, the page calls fetch and sends a request to a URL with controllable input that is not encoded in the path. This allows you to inject "../" sequences and steer the request to an arbitrary endpoint - this is CSPT.

If you steer the fetch to an endpoint that returns controllable content (for example, JS with text-injection), and that content ends up in the DOM / gets executed, you get XSS from CSPT.

entries.title: CSPT to XSS - exploitation example
entries.body:
• The page https://example.com/static/cms/news.html accepts a newsitemid parameter
• It then fetches the content of https://example.com/newitems/<newsitemid>
• Separately, a text-injection was found in https://example.com/pricing/default.js via the cb parameter
• Final payload (newsitemid with "../" steers the fetch to the vulnerable default.js):

https://example.com/static/cms/news.html?newsitemid=../pricing/default.js?cb=alert(document.domain)//

entries.title: CSPT to CSRF - the essence of the technique
entries.body:
CSPT redirects legitimate HTTP requests. The frontend itself adds the necessary tokens to such requests (authentication, CSRF token). If you steer a request via "../" to a "sink" endpoint that performs an action, you can bypass existing CSRF protection.

This is exactly why CSPT2CSRF works where ordinary CSRF is powerless: the client itself inserts the tokens, and SameSite=Lax does not get in the way (the request originates from within the site itself).

rows: Impact depends on source and sink?

entries.title: CSPT2CSRF with a POST sink - Mattermost (CVE-2023-45316)
Real-world CSPT2CSRF cases
entries.body:
• 1-click CSPT2CSRF in Rocket.Chat.
• CVE-2023-45316 - CSPT2CSRF with a POST sink in Mattermost.
• CVE-2023-6458 - CSPT2CSRF with a GET sink in Mattermost.
• CVE-2023-5123 - CSPT2CSRF in Grafana JSON API Plugin.
• Client Side Path Manipulation (erasec.be) - CSPT2CSRF via the invite flow.

The pattern: the impact of CSPT2CSRF depends on the pair source (where the path injection is) → sink (which action endpoint the request can be steered to).

entries.title: Tools and platforms for CSPT
entries.body:
• doyensec/CSPTBurpExtension - a Burp Suite extension for finding and exploiting Client-Side Path Traversal.
• doyensec/CSPTPlayground - an open playground for finding and exploiting CSPT.
• Root Me - CSPT - The Ruler - a CSPT lab challenge.
